source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
3.1. The features of the CSCS and their impact for corporations
The data for the CSCS is derived from very diverse sources. Next to inspec-
tions and data transmitted by the companies themselves, the CSCS uses
video surveillance and the data of third parties such as Alibaba or Tencent
to monitor and consequently rate the corporations.66 In addition to this
mass of data and concerns about data security and access,67 Hoﬀman
shows in her analysis that the ‘market and legal data that make up a
person’s or entity’s credit record is intrinsically political, while input
sources can be simultaneously political and non-political’.68
   Data, which are largely based on reputation, eg, derived from traditional
and social media, contribute to the scoring.69 This factor calls into question
the objectiveness of the system. When non-Chinese companies issued state-
ments or posts in the past, which were not in line with the CCP foreign
policy, outraged reactions of social media users caused these foreign
64
   See Cutler (n 8) 722.
65
   Ibid, 723.
66
   See European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 13–16.
67
   This mass of data from various sources highlights the necessity of the safe storage and security of data.
   Previous research indicates that there is no guarantee and no clear legislation preventing the state to
   use its position to gain unwanted access, see Stein van Deursen, HRBM Kummeling, ‘The New Silk
   Road: a bumpy ride for Sino-European collaborative research under the GDPR?’ (2019) 78(5) Higher
   Education 911 and also, Jiang, Fu (n 30). The various data sets taken in isolation might not be a
   problem for corporate data integrity but taken together the mass of data could lead to exposure
   of sensitive data points, see European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 4. This can be
   convenient for the state and its intelligence agencies (as in case of the corporation SWIFT in the
   context of gathering intelligence for ‘the war on terror’ by the US and Europe (Henry Farrell and
   Abraham Newman, ‘Weaponized Interdependence: How Global Economic Networks Shape Global
   Coercion’ (2019) 44(1) International Security 42, 65–70) but it also raises concern with regard to
   third actors gaining access. In Shanghai the state entrusted a private credit company to develop
   the credit scores for Shanghai citizens (see Chen and Cheung (n 29) 369). A certain risk of fraud
   and data manipulation exists as there are no suﬃcient checks and balances at the moment, see
   Fei Shen (n 37) 30.
68
   See Hoﬀman (n 11) 6.
69
   See European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 23.
 304         S. JAKOB
